questions.question: Which naval battle in 1571 stopped the Ottoman Empire's westward expansion in the Mediterranean, credited with saving Western Europe from Ottoman conquest?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Lepanto (October 7, 1571) was the largest naval battle in Western history to that point. The Holy League (Spain, Venice, Papal States, Genoa) defeated the Ottoman fleet, destroying over 200 ships. It ended Ottoman naval dominance in the Mediterranean. Miguel de Cervantes (later author of Don Quixote) was wounded in the battle.
questions.memory_hook: Lepanto 1571: Holy League stopped Ottoman westward expansion. Cervantes (Don Quixote) was wounded there.

questions.question: What is the name of the dense, hot region at the center of our Sun where nuclear fusion occurs, generating the Sun's energy?
questions.explanation: The Sun's core extends to about 25% of the Sun's radius and reaches 15 million degrees Celsius. Here, 620 million tons of hydrogen are fused into helium every second, releasing energy according to E=mc². It takes energy about 100,000 years to travel from the core to the photosphere (surface).
questions.memory_hook: Solar core: 15M°C. Fuses 620M tons of hydrogen per second. Takes 100,000 years for energy to reach surface.

questions.question: In baseball, which statistic measures the effectiveness of starting pitchers, calculated as total earned runs allowed per nine innings pitched?
questions.explanation: ERA measures runs scored without the help of errors over 9 innings. A sub-3.00 ERA is considered excellent; sub-2.00 is historic. Bob Gibson's 1968 ERA of 1.12 remains a modern record. WHIP (walks plus hits per inning pitched) is a more modern metric; WAR (wins above replacement) is now the most comprehensive single statistic.
questions.memory_hook: ERA: earned runs per 9 innings. Sub-3.00 = excellent. Bob Gibson's 1.12 ERA in 1968 is legendary.

questions.question: Which punk rock band, formed in London in 1975 and fronted by Johnny Rotten (John Lydon), had a massive cultural impact with their 1977 album 'Never Mind the Bollocks'?
questions.explanation: The Sex Pistols existed for about three years but fundamentally changed music. Their confrontational approach, DIY aesthetic, and anti-establishment attitude launched the punk movement. Never Mind the Bollocks was banned by UK stores but still reached #1. Sid Vicious became an icon; John Lydon later spoke out against the nostalgia industry.
questions.memory_hook: Sex Pistols: 3-year band that launched punk. 'Never Mind the Bollocks' banned but #1. Never Mind the Bollocks reached #1.

questions.question: Which landlocked country in Central Asia, a former Soviet republic, is home to the Aral Sea disaster — once the world's fourth-largest lake, now largely desert?
questions.explanation: The Aral Sea straddles Kazakhstan and Uzbekistan. Soviet irrigation projects in the 1960s diverted the Amu Darya and Syr Darya rivers, depriving the lake of water. By 2007, it had shrunk to 10% of its original size — one of the worst environmental disasters in history. Former fishing villages now sit 150 km from water.
questions.memory_hook: Aral Sea: on Kazakhstan/Uzbekistan border. Soviet irrigation dried it to 10% of original size. Fishing boats stranded in desert.

questions.question: Which ancient Egyptian monument is the only surviving ancient wonder of the world?
questions.explanation: The Great Pyramid of Giza, built for Pharaoh Khufu around 2560 BC, stood as the world's tallest structure for 3,800 years (until Lincoln Cathedral in 1311 AD). It contains approximately 2.3 million stone blocks weighing 2.5-15 tons each. How ancient Egyptians built it with such precision remains debated, though it clearly required massive organized labor.
questions.memory_hook: Great Pyramid: only surviving Ancient Wonder. World's tallest structure for 3,800 years. 2.3 million blocks.

questions.question: Which billionaire entrepreneur founded PayPal, Tesla, SpaceX, and also appeared in a cameo role in the Marvel Cinematic Universe's Iron Man 2?
questions.explanation: Elon Musk appeared briefly in Iron Man 2 (2010) as himself at a party scene, complimenting Tony Stark on a clean energy concept. This was a nod to the real Musk's Iron Man-like public persona — Jon Favreau based aspects of Tony Stark on Musk. Musk has also made cameos in The Big Bang Theory, South Park, and other productions.
questions.memory_hook: Musk appeared in Iron Man 2 as himself. Jon Favreau partly based Tony Stark on Musk.

questions.question: What is the term for the amount of heat required to raise the temperature of 1 gram of water by 1 degree Celsius?
questions.explanation: The small calorie (cal) is the energy to raise 1g of water by 1°C. The kilocalorie (kcal) or Calorie (capital C) is 1,000 small calories and is what food labels list. A joule is the SI unit of energy (1 cal = 4.184 joules). A BTU (British Thermal Unit) raises 1 pound of water by 1°F.
questions.memory_hook: Calorie (small): raises 1g water by 1°C. Food Calories = kilocalories (1,000x). 1 cal = 4.184 joules.

questions.question: Which U.S. state, the largest by area, is separated from the contiguous 48 states and is home to the highest peak in North America, Denali?
questions.explanation: Alaska covers 1.72 million km² — larger than the next three largest U.S. states combined. Denali (formerly known as Mt. McKinley) stands at 6,190 meters. Alaska has more coastline than all other U.S. states combined, the largest national parks, and about half the world's glaciers. It was purchased from Russia in 1867 for $7.2 million.
questions.memory_hook: Alaska: largest US state. Denali = 6,190m, highest in North America. Bought from Russia 1867 for $7.2M.

questions.question: Which organization, founded in 1945 after World War II, replaced the League of Nations and now has 193 member states?
questions.explanation: The United Nations was established October 24, 1945 (UN Day), with 51 founding member states. Its headquarters are in New York City (on land donated by John D. Rockefeller Jr.). The UN has six principal organs including the Security Council (with 5 permanent veto-holding members: US, UK, France, Russia, China).
questions.memory_hook: UN: founded Oct 24, 1945. 51 founding members, 193 now. HQ in NYC. 5 permanent Security Council members.

questions.question: Which singer, born in Memphis and known as the 'King of Rock and Roll,' had his first hit 'Heartbreak Hotel' in 1956 and influenced generations of musicians?
questions.explanation: Elvis Presley (1935-1977) combined Black rhythm and blues with country and gospel for a new sound. His hip movements on The Ed Sullivan Show were so provocative he was only filmed from the waist up. He sold over 500 million records globally. He died at 42 from prescription drug use. Graceland is the second most-visited home in the US (after the White House).
questions.memory_hook: Elvis: King of Rock and Roll. Shot from waist up on Ed Sullivan. Died at 42. Graceland: 2nd most visited US home.

questions.question: What is the name of the prestigious international golf tournament held each year in Augusta, Georgia, known for its green jacket awarded to the winner?
questions.explanation: The Masters Tournament (officially the Augusta National Golf Club Invitational) is held every April at Augusta National. The green jacket has been awarded since 1949. Tiger Woods won it 5 times (1997, 2001, 2002, 2005, 2019). Jack Nicklaus holds the record with 6 wins. The course's back nine on Sunday is called 'Amen Corner' for its dramatic holes.
questions.memory_hook: The Masters: Augusta, April, green jacket. Nicklaus: 6 wins. Tiger: 5. 'Amen Corner' is the famous back nine stretch.
